does: This paper measures which defense families in production LLM applications effectively mitigate which threats from the OWASP-LLM-Top-10 list. The authors deploy a breach-and-attack-simulation (BAS) benchmark with 25 total agents (21 baseline plus 4 new OWASP-aware agents) against a lattice of four synthetic LLM endpoints: undefended, refusal-phrase filtered, token-budget controlled, or a full-stack combination. Across 10 replications, they provide precise attribution: refusal filters eliminate jailbreak and system-prompt leakage findings, token budgets eliminate sensitive-data disclosure and unbounded-consumption findings by truncating multi-step attacks, while excessive-agency threats require the full defensive stack. They additionally evaluate defense brittleness when prompts are paraphrased, revealing where protections remain robust versus fragile.
connects: This work extends breach-and-attack-simulation benchmarking and OWASP security frameworks (previously applied to web applications and now adapted to LLMs) by introducing fine-grained attribution analysis. Prior BAS research reported aggregate coverage metrics without explaining threat-defense mapping, and earlier LLM-security work treated defenses monolithically. This paper builds on growing literature documenting individual vulnerabilities—jailbreaks, prompt injection, information disclosure, and agentic overreach—by systematically isolating which defense families address which specific threats. The lattice design (non-subset sibling ablations plus unions) advances experimental methodology beyond simple on-off toggles, enabling cleaner causal attribution. The paraphrasing robustness evaluation responds to emerging concerns about defense evasion through synonym substitution and reformulation.
enables: This attribution framework enables practitioners to allocate defensive resources more efficiently, knowing precisely which threat categories each defense family handles and which require layered protections. The paraphrasing-brittleness results point toward future work on robust defusal mechanisms, potentially through semantic rather than syntactic filtering or adversarial training. The clean separation of threat-defense mappings provides a foundation for formal verification research: can one prove that budget controls guarantee termination before multi-step attacks succeed, or characterize conditions under which refusal filters remain effective under paraphrasing? The work also enables benchmarking of new defenses against a standardized, fine-grained threat model, supporting the development of more sophisticated protections for threats like excessive agency that currently require full stacks.
